abstract:
Finding the maximum independent set (MIS) is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications. In recent years, branch-and-reduce algorithms—which integrate reduction rules into a branch-and-bound framework—have become the state-of-the-art for solving MIS exactly, particularly on large-scale, practical instances. While significant progress has been driven by the discovery of increasingly powerful reduction rules, other critical algorithmic components have remained relatively under-explored. One such component is the branching strategy, which dictates the order in which vertices are processed. Although selecting the vertex of the highest degree has long been the standard heuristic, its performance is often suboptimal.

In this work, we introduce a novel branching strategy guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Due to the structural complexity of modern solvers, traditional supervised and reinforcement learning techniques are difficult to implement in this context. To overcome this, we employ a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s parameters directly toward faster execution times. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of this learning-based approach, achieving a speedup on 73% of benchmark instances with a median improvement of 24%.
